original_abstract:
This study presents a comparative investigation of granular activated carbon (GAC) filtration and ion exchange (IEX) treatment for the removal of short- and long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in a waterworks setting. Through laboratory batch experiments and pilot-scale column tests, we characterized the performance of various adsorbents within a PFAS-polluted groundwater matrix. Across all materials, the sorption affinity for short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) was significantly lower than that of their long-chain counterparts or short-chain perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs).

In pilot trials, GAC exhibited limited efficacy for the mobile short-chain compounds PFBA and PFPeA, reaching 10% breakthrough at relatively low throughputs of 6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes (BV), respectively. While ion exchange media demonstrated superior longevity in some cases—reaching 10% breakthrough at 5,000 BV for PFBA, 10,000 BV for PFPeA, and 18,000 BV for PFHxA—the higher unit cost of IEX resins prevents them from being a more economical "single-use" alternative to GAC.

However, the economic viability of IEX improves significantly when incorporating resin regeneration. We demonstrate that while long-chain PFAS require the addition of ethanol to the regeneration brine for effective elution, short-chain species like PFBA and PFPeA can be recovered from weakly basic ion exchangers using aqueous regenerants alone. These findings suggest that regenerated IEX systems may offer a more sustainable and cost-effective strategy for managing complex PFAS plumes than traditional carbon filtration.
humanized_abstract:
Short- and long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are a group of persistent groundwater contaminants of emerging concern. In this study, short- and long-chain PFAS removal efficiency using GAC filtration and IEX treatment was investigated in waterworks scenario. Laboratory batch experiments and pilot-scale column tests were carried out using a range of adsorbents in a contaminated groundwater matrix. Overall, regardless of the type of adsorbent used, it was observed that the short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) had lower sorption affinity than their long-chain homologues and short-chain perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs).

In the pilot trials, GAC was ineffective for the mobile short-chain compounds PFBA and PFPeA. The breakthrough levels were 10% at relatively low throughputs of 6000 and 11000 bed volumes (BV), respectively. Although there were instances where the ion exchange media was significantly more effective – for example, the 10% breakthrough was at 5000 BV for PFBA, 10000 BV for PFPeA and 18000 BV for PFHxA – their higher unit cost makes them an unattractive, if not economically speaking, a "single-use" alternative to GAC.

An economically viable process using the IEX system for wastewater treatment is obtained after integrating the resin regeneration stage. Moreover, while the need to add ethanol to the regeneration brine to remove the long-chain PFAS complicates the process for removal of these contaminants, the short-chain PFAS such as PFBA and PFPeA can be removed from the weakly basic ion exchangers using aqueous regeneration solutions without the need for catalysts or additional chemicals, which makes the regenerated IEX system for removal of these contaminants more economical and sustainable than activated carbon systems for the treatment of the complex PFAS plumes that are difficult to manage.

document.input:
This study presents a comparative investigation of granular activated carbon (GAC) filtration and ion exchange (IEX) treatment for the removal of short- and long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in a waterworks setting.
